title: Celtis pubescens
taxonomy: Celtis pubescens is classified as a species within the genus Celtis, which is a part of the family Cannabaceae. However, it is also sometimes placed in the family Ulmaceae. The genus Celtis is a group of deciduous trees and shrubs commonly known as hackberries or nettle trees. This classification may vary depending on the source or taxonomic system used. Celtis pubescens is native to South America.
uses: Celtis pubescens, also known as the Mexican hackberry, has been traditionally used in various medicinal practices. The plant's bark, leaves, and roots have been employed for treating various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. Its antiseptic properties have been utilized to treat wounds and prevent infections. Traditional healers also use Celtis pubescens to treat digestive issues and promote overall health. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using the plant for medicinal purposes.
benefits: Celtis pubescens, also known as the pubescent hackberry, has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its potential health benefits. The plant's bark, leaves, and roots are believed to possess anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties. Its extracts have been studied for their potential in treating various health conditions, including fever, digestive issues, and skin problems. Furthermore, Celtis pubescens is thought to have antioxidant effects that may help protect against oxidative stress and cell damage. However, more research is needed to confirm the plant's efficacy and safety as a medicinal agent.

constituents: Celtis pubescens, also known as the southern hackberry or sugarberry, is a plant with various active constituents. These active constituents include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and tannins, which may provide antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. parts: Celtis pubescens, which is also known as the Chittim pear or Desert hackberry, has been used in traditional medicine.
